content.assessment: Viewed through the whole-system reform lens, this proposal is a within-track admissions fix rather than a structural or comprehensive equity bundle. It attempts to reduce family-capital sorting at a single selection juncture (age 10/12 gimnázium entry) without addressing the other selection points identified in the evidence record, particularly primary-school choice segregation, which occurs prior to and independently of gimnázium admission. The comparative record indicates that isolated interventions of this kind have historically underperformed relative to coordinated, multi-lever reforms (unified curricula, extended comprehensive schooling, catchment coordination). The proposal may reduce coaching-driven sorting at one gate but leaves the broader system architecture, and the earlier segregation mechanism, untouched.
opportunities: Could serve as one component of a larger coordinated bundle if paired with primary-choice catchment reform and quality-levelling measures
Outreach/registration mandates could partially address disadvantaged-school non-participation, a distinct equity lever from admission-rule design
strengths: Targets a specific, well-evidenced sorting mechanism (coaching-return, interview subjectivity) at the formal admissions gate
Builds on existing regulatory levers (centralized algorithm, interview weight caps) rather than requiring new institutional architecture, plausibly easing implementation
threats: Elite family adaptation to new exam formats is a recognized risk pattern in reform literature, potentially eroding gains over time
Absent structural change, the underlying sorting incentive (departure of high performers into a still-selective track) persists, limiting the reform's systemic reach
weaknesses: Does not address segregation originating in primary-school free choice, a documented independent mechanism operating before age-12 tracking
Non-structural admissions tweaks are not shown in the comparative record to achieve durable equity gains without accompanying curricular or structural bundling
